explicit_text: Amelia Island was the place where Major James Pilot lost all his negroes when they escaped on board a British warship around 1800. It became a settlement area for families like the Pilots, Vaughans, and Harrisons after they left the unhealthy rice fields near Darien, Georgia. The island saw plantations established near Fernandina and Amelia City, with Sam Harrison being the first to settle at the island's end. The island also witnessed the destruction of fine old plantation homes during the War between the States.
implicit_text: The island was where I lost all my negroes when they escaped on board a British warship around 1800. It became a settlement area for families like mine, the Vaughans, and Harrisons after we left the unhealthy rice fields near Darien, Georgia. Plantations were established near Fernandina and the island's main city, with Sam Harrison being the first to settle at the island's end. The island also witnessed the destruction of fine old plantation homes during the War between the States.

explicit_text: Around 1800, a British warship played a significant role in Major James Pilot's life when all his negroes escaped by boarding it right on Amelia Island. This event was a major loss for Major Pilot and marked a turning point in his holdings. The British warship was thus indirectly involved in the shifting ownership and control of plantations in the region during that period.
implicit_text: Around 1800, a British warship played a significant role in my life when all my negroes escaped by boarding it right on the island where I lived. This event was a major loss for me and marked a turning point in my holdings. The British warship was thus indirectly involved in the shifting ownership and control of plantations in the region during that period.

explicit_text: The United States was involved in the loss Major James Pilot suffered in 1812 when soldiers took all his property and negroes. Later, in 1836, the United States Government reimbursed his family $26,000 for this loss, which was known as the 'Spanish Claim.' The United States census of 1796 also records Major James Pilot and his family living in South Carolina, showing his early ties to the country.

explicit_text: The Spanish Claim refers to the reimbursement Major James Pilot's family received in 1836 from the United States Government for losses suffered in 1812 when soldiers took their property and negroes. This payment of $26,000 was a partial compensation for those losses. The claim was well known in the family and marked an important legal and financial event in their history.

explicit_text: I am Major James Pilot, son of Rev. Jamie Pilot, born on my father's plantation near Charleston, South Carolina, called 'Wreherode.' I fought in the Revolutionary War and was taken prisoner at Furiburg until the war ended. By 1796, I was living in South Carolina with my family and slaves, and by 1807, I had a large Spanish land grant on Aiken's Island near Amelia Island. My plantation neighbors included John Vaughan and Robert Harrison, and our families were closely connected by marriage.

My plantation neighbors included John Vaughan and Robert Harrison, and our families were closely connected by marriage.

explicit_text: I am Rev. Jamie Pilot, father of Major James Pilot, and once a wealthy plantation owner near Charleston, South Carolina. Later in life, I became a famous preacher and founded one of the largest churches in Charleston. My plantation was called 'Wreherode,' where my son James was born before he went off to fight in the Revolutionary War.

explicit_text: Charleston, South Carolina, was the home of Rev. Jamie Pilot and his plantation called 'Wreherode,' where Major James Pilot was born. It was a significant place for the Pilot family, as Rev. Jamie Pilot later became a famous preacher there and built one of the largest churches in the city. The area was also central to the family's early life before the Revolutionary War.

explicit_text: During the Revolutionary War, I was taken prisoner at Furiburg and held there until the war ended. This was a difficult time, but it marked my commitment to the cause. Furiburg was thus a significant location in my military service and personal history.

explicit_text: South Carolina was where I lived in 1796 with my wife, children, and thirteen slaves, as recorded in the United States census. It was also the home state of my father, Rev. Jamie Pilot, and the place where my brother, Major Charles Pilot, also lived with his family and slaves. South Carolina was the starting point before we moved to Spanish land grants near Amelia Island.

South Carolina was the starting point before we moved to Spanish land grants near the island where I later settled.

explicit_text: By 1807, I and my son Francis were living on Aiken's Island, where I had a very large land grant from the Spanish Government. My plantation there adjoined those of John Vaughan and Robert Harrison, and our families were closely connected through marriage. Aiken's Island was an important part of our family's plantation holdings in the early 19th century.

explicit_text: John Vaughan was my neighbor on Aiken's Island, where our plantations adjoined. Our families, including the Vaughans and Pilots, were intermarried and connected to many prominent Southern families. The Vaughan family also had ties to old Savannah families, strengthening our social and economic networks in the region.

explicit_text: Robert Harrison was one of the plantation owners neighboring Major James Pilot on Aiken's Island, holding a 500-acre grant. He was part of the Harrison family, which intermarried with the Pilots and Vaughans, forming a close-knit community of plantation owners. His descendants, including a Major Robert Harrison who fought in the Confederate Army, continued the family's legacy on Amelia Island.

explicit_text: The Harrison family were prominent plantation owners near Amelia Island, with a 500-acre grant adjacent to the Pilot and Vaughan lands. They intermarried with these families and were connected to many of the finest Southern families. The Harrisons had a rich history, including military service in both the English and Confederate armies, and their plantations were among the finest in the South before being destroyed during the War between the States.

explicit_text: The Vaughan and Pilot families were tied up with old Savannah families, which helped establish their social standing and connections in the South. Savannah was part of the broader regional network that linked these plantation families. These ties influenced marriages and land holdings around Amelia Island and Georgia.

explicit_text: Darien, Georgia, was near where early settlers like the Pilots, Vaughans, Harrisons, Browards, and Thorpes lived around 1790. The land there was swampy and unhealthy, which led these families to leave their rice fields and move to Amelia Island and nearby areas. Darien was also the hometown of Mary Cooper, who married into the Harrison family.
implicit_text: Darien, Georgia, was near where early settlers like my family, the Vaughans, Harrisons, Browards, and Thorpes lived around 1790. The land there was swampy and unhealthy, which led us to leave our rice fields and move to the island and nearby areas. Darien was also the hometown of Mary Cooper, who married into the Harrison family.

explicit_text: Fernandina, Florida, was the little Spanish city where settlers like the Pilots, Vaughans, and Harrisons landed when they migrated from Georgia. There, they met Spanish officers and obtained land grants, which required them to live on the land for a certain time to secure final titles from the Spanish Government at St. Augustine. Fernandina was thus a gateway for these families to establish plantations on Amelia Island.

Fernandina was thus a gateway for these families to establish plantations on the island.

explicit_text: The Spanish Government granted large tracts of land to settlers like Major James Pilot and his son Francis on Aiken's Island around 1807. They required settlers to live on the land for a certain period before granting final titles, which were issued through representatives in St. Augustine. These grants were crucial for establishing plantations on Amelia Island and surrounding areas.

explicit_text: St. Augustine was the place where Spanish Government representatives issued final land titles to settlers like the Pilots, Vaughans, and Harrisons after they fulfilled residency requirements. This city played a key administrative role in legitimizing land ownership on Amelia Island and nearby areas during the early 19th century.
implicit_text: St. Augustine was the place where Spanish Government representatives issued final land titles to settlers like my family and the Vaughans and Harrisons after we fulfilled residency requirements. This city played a key administrative role in legitimizing land ownership on the island and nearby areas during the early 19th century.

explicit_text: Eli Whitney, who invented the cotton gin around 1790 or 1792, is believed to have visited Cumberland Island near Fernandina during that time. It is probable he met plantation owners like the Harrisons and that one of his early cotton-separating machines was used on the Harrison plantation. This connection highlights the technological advancements influencing Southern plantations.

This connection highlights the technological advancements influencing Southern plantations.

explicit_text: Cumberland Island, located across from Fernandina, Florida, was visited by Eli Whitney around 1790 or 1792. It was near the plantations owned by families like the Harrisons, and Whitney likely met these plantation owners during his visit. The island was thus part of the regional context for early cotton gin use and plantation agriculture.

explicit_text: Jacksonville, Florida, is where Mrs. Ann Harrison, a descendant of Sam Harrison, lives. I turned over transcripts of old Harrison family papers to her, which included testimony about the plantation's crops and finances. Jacksonville thus serves as a modern connection to the Harrison family's historical legacy on Amelia Island.

explicit_text: Mary Cooper married Robert Harrison's son, who was a major, linking the Cooper and Harrison families. She was the sister of General James Cooper, who married a Vaughan girl, further intertwining these prominent families. Mary Cooper brought with her a personal maid who had been her own, showing the close personal and social ties among these families.

His family ties helped consolidate the social and landholding networks among these prominent Southern families. He was a significant figure in the family's history during the early 19th century.

explicit_text: Hebraia Morrison was a granddaughter of the Harrison family who married Attis Cooper, linking the Harrisons and Coopers further. Their son was Sam Harrison, Sr., continuing the family legacy. Hebraia's marriage represents the interconnection of prominent families in the region.

explicit_text: Colonel John Cooper was the father of General James Cooper and father-in-law to Robert Harrison's son. He was involved in a trust deed made by Major Robert Harrison in 1810, which placed plantations and slaves in trust for Mary Cooper upon marriage. Colonel John Cooper was a key figure in managing family estates and protecting property rights during that era.

explicit_text: Sam Harrison was one of the sons of the first Harrison who divided up the family land on Amelia Island. He chose to settle right at the end of the island, making his plantation the first among the group. His descendants, including Robert and Abraham Harrison, continued the family's presence and influence in the area.

explicit_text: Colonel Sam Harrison, a descendant of the Harrison family, served in the United States Army and was stationed in the Philippines. This military service continued the family's tradition of involvement in national affairs. The United States Army thus represents a modern extension of the Harrison family's legacy.

explicit_text: Major Robert Harrison, a son of the Harrison family, served with distinction in the Confederate States Army during the War between the States. After the war, he spent the rest of his life on the old family plantations on Amelia Island. His service reflects the family's deep involvement in Southern military history.

explicit_text: The War between the States had a profound impact on our plantations on Amelia Island, as the fine old homes were destroyed by Northern troops during their visits. Major Robert Harrison served with distinction in the Confederate Army during this conflict. After the war, he deeded land to loyal former slaves, establishing communities like Franklintown.

explicit_text: Franklintown, Florida, is the area on Amelia Island where Major Robert Harrison deeded part of his land to faithful former slaves after the War between the States. Descendants of these slaves still live there today, including Norris Drummond, whose family has maintained the land for generations. Franklintown represents a lasting legacy of loyalty and community on the island.
